Jan 15, 2025

Iger\xe2\x80\x99s Fubo Curveball, L.A.\xe2\x80\x99s Political Fire, TV After Broadcast

Welcome back to The Daily Courant, your afternoon digest of Puck\xe2\x80\x99s best new reporting.

Today, we lead with Eriq Gardner \xe2\x80\x99s incisive summation of two legal dramas roiling the worlds of media and entertainment: The Times \xe2\x80\x99 high-profile copyright battle against Sam Altman \xe2\x80\x99s OpenAI, and the Supreme Court\xe2\x80\x99s imminent decision on the fate of TikTok. Will the social media phenomenon survive the weekend?

Plus, below the fold: Marion Maneker digs into an art world micro-scandal surrounding the estate of Peter Beard . Sarah Shapiro talks to Hill House Home boss Nell Diamond about the Pyrrhic success of the company\xe2\x80\x99s category-defining Nap Dress. Tara Palmeri and former Trump official Ken Cuccinelli debate the viability of the next administration\xe2\x80\x99s deportation strategy. And Julie Davich chronicles a surprising development in the art brokerage business.


Meanwhile, on the podcasts: Democratic rising star Mallory McMorrow joins John Heilemann on Impolitic to investigate a Trumpworld mystery. On The Varsity , Sara Fischer joins John Ourand to contemplate the ripple effects of a TikTok ban on sports media. On The Town , Matt Belloni connects with CBS Entertainment president Amy Reisenbach to envision the end state of broadcast television. And on The Powers That Be , Dylan Byers and Peter Hamby examine L.A. mayor Karen Bass \xe2\x80\x99s bleak political future.

MEDIA

Eriq Gardner

TikTok\xe2\x80\x99s Odds & An A.I. Suit for the Ages

On Tuesday, lawyers for OpenAI urged a judge to dismiss key claims in The New York Times Company\xe2\x80\x99s high-profile lawsuit against Sam Altman\xe2\x80\x99s $157 billion A.I. lab. Yes, it\xe2\x80\x99s just one of more than 35 major lawsuits poised to set the legal table for the future of generative A.I., but this case is notable for a number of reasons\xe2\x80\x94not least of which is the fact that the Times is represented by Susman Godfrey, a firm skilled at netting colossal paydays. Eriq evaluates the potential outcomes before turning to the odds that TikTok\xe2\x80\x99s Chinese parent, ByteDance, will be forced to divest from the social media platform before the weekend is up. While there are signs suggesting the ban will be upheld by the Supreme Court, don\xe2\x80\x99t expect a bold, sweeping opinion. This could easily become a fractured ruling with concurring opinions galore, leaving little in the way of clear precedent. And, of course, Trump remains a wild card\xe2\x80\xa6

Read Now

ART

Marion Maneker

Policing Peter Beard\xe2\x80\x99s Legacy

Last month, lawyers for the late artist Peter Beard\xe2\x80\x99s studio, run by his widow, Nejma, filed a lawsuit against Heritage Auctions, disputing the sale of 51 lots held in October. The studio argues that the consignor never had a right to sell the items, and is upset that Heritage didn\xe2\x80\x99t consult with them about the nature of the materials before the sale, as many of the lots were manuscripts or personal photographs, diary pages, or even books. Interestingly, the studio seems most concerned with asserting its role as the arbiter of what is, and what is not, a Beard work of art. The strength and quality of an artist\xe2\x80\x99s studio or foundation can be the difference between their legacy dissipating into the ether or gathering strength with time. And soon enough, Beard\xe2\x80\x99s own fate might be decided.

Read Now

FASHION

Sarah Shapiro

Is There Life After the Nap Dress?

Nell Diamond made one of the more fortuitous pivots in recent fashion business history when she redirected her somnolent D.T.C. bedding brand, Hill House Home, toward a singular blockbuster product that defined the pandemic era and now comprises more than 20 percent of the company\xe2\x80\x99s revenue: the Nap Dress. It transformed the business, and helped Hill House Home generate more revenue in a single day in 2021 than in all of 2019. The knock on Diamond, of course, is that her company hasn\xe2\x80\x99t evolved beyond the Nap Dress or its current, online-only Shopbop and Saks partnerships. Most brands dream of a product that can define a category, as Hill House did with its Nap Dress. Fashion, however, is about the future. So what does Diamond have planned for an encore?

Read Now

WASHINGTON

Tara Palmeri

\xe2\x80\x9cThe Largest Deportation Effort of Our Lifetime\xe2\x80\x9d

One of Trump\xe2\x80\x99s most quoted campaign promises was to launch, on day one, \xe2\x80\x9cthe largest deportation program of criminals in the history of America.\xe2\x80\x9d But, perhaps needless to say, he\xe2\x80\x99s not going to be able to remove millions of illegal immigrants in a week, or even in a year. Actually removing people is the hypothetical end point of what will inevitably be a dragged-out, vociferous, politically fraught, and logistically complex process involving warrants, identification, surveillance, detention, and many, many lawyers. For a candid perspective on how it would actually work\xe2\x80\x94and what it might cost\xe2\x80\x94Tara spoke with Ken Cuccinelli, a former Virginia attorney general and Trump\xe2\x80\x99s former acting number two at the Department of Homeland Security.

Read Now

ART

Julie Davich

An Art World Brokerage Play

Gurr Johns\xe2\x80\x99 core business for the past 110 years has been appraisals\xe2\x80\x94a service many collectors view as a necessary evil to placate their insurance companies and tax advisors, rather than as an investment of sorts. Now, all that is changing: New C.E.O. Maarten ten Holder wants to expand the firm\xe2\x80\x99s advisory services\xe2\x80\x94which comprise only a small part of the business and are usually just an upsell on the appraisal relationships\xe2\x80\x94and is looking to launch a brokerage offering, too. Gurr Johns built its core business on the trust of clients who largely don\xe2\x80\x99t want the auction houses to know what\xe2\x80\x99s in their portfolio and make deals on that information. Will this new brokerage business undercut that positioning?

Read Now

WASHINGTON

John Heilemann

Mallory McMorrow: Women Are (Still) Pissed\xe2\x80\x94But Also Patient

John is joined by Democratic Michigan state senator Mallory McMorrow to discuss the commencement of Trump 2.0 and where her party goes from here. Mallory opines on the Pete Hegseth confirmation hearing and explains why women are greeting Trump\xe2\x80\x99s second term so differently than his first, despite the profusion of appointees and advisors accused of sexual misconduct; the importance of paying less attention to what Trump says and more to what he actually does; why Kamala Harris lost and Joe Biden never should have sought a second term; and what her party needs to do to start winning again.

Listen Now

SPORTS

John Ourand

Iger\xe2\x80\x99s Fubo Curveball & The TikTok Endgame

Axios\xe2\x80\x99s Sara Fischer joins John for a wide-ranging conversation covering the latest trends transforming the sports media landscape: the striking Disney-Fubo merger, DirecTV\xe2\x80\x99s new $70/month sports package, the potential ripple effects of a TikTok ban on sports media, and the murky future of R.S.N.s in major markets like Chicago and New York.

Listen Now

HOLLYWOOD

Matthew Belloni

Why Broadcast Television Won\xe2\x80\x99t Die

Matt is joined by Amy Reisenbach, the president of CBS Entertainment, to talk about how broadcast networks continue to succeed despite living in a world of streaming and social media. She discusses why these linear networks are still surviving, how a hybrid broadcast-streaming model can be beneficial for legacy networks, and the challenge of programming shows for audiences of different age ranges on linear versus streaming. Matt finishes the show with a prediction about a new sports bundle from DirecTV.

Listen Now

WASHINGTON

Peter Hamby
Dylan Byers

L.A.\xe2\x80\x99s Karen Bass Problem & Rashida\xe2\x80\x99s MSNBC Exit

Dylan Byers joins Peter to reflect on the ongoing wildfire disaster in Los Angeles and the political blame game that\xe2\x80\x99s only just begun\xe2\x80\x94underscored by Mayor Karen Bass\xe2\x80\x99s perplexing response to the crisis. The duo also discuss MSNBC president Rashida Jones\xe2\x80\x99s foreshadowed departure from the network and who could potentially replace her.
